topic: summer heat affecting tire blowout rates

turns.content: so summer is coming up and every year I see a big spike in blowouts. trying to get ahead of it this year. what should I be telling customers to watch for

Smart to get ahead of it. Heat is the number one killer of tires — hot pavement can reach 150+ degrees in summer, and that combined with underinflated tires is the recipe for blowouts.

Here's what to hammer home with customers:

1. **Check tire pressure monthly** — and check it when the tires are cold (before driving). Heat expands air, so a tire that reads fine after driving might actually be low.
2. **Look at tread depth** — anything under 4/32" going into summer is risky. The thinner the tread, the less heat the tire can dissipate.
3. **Watch for sidewall damage** — bulges, cracks, or cuts. Summer heat accelerates failure on damaged sidewalls.
4. **Don't overload the vehicle** — extra weight plus heat is a bad combo.

Are you thinking about running a seasonal inspection special or something like that?
yeah thats not a bad idea actually. maybe a free tire check or something to get people in the door

Free tire inspections are one of the best door-openers in the business. You'll spend maybe 10 minutes per car checking pressure, tread depth, and visual condition. And a good chunk of those people will need tires, an alignment, or a rotation right there on the spot.

You could call it a "Summer Safety Check" or something simple. Run it through May and June before the real heat kicks in. Put a sign out front, maybe post about it if you have any social media presence.

The key is catching tires that are borderline BEFORE they blow out on somebody. Customers remember the shop that told them "hey, you need to replace these before summer" — especially when their neighbor has a blowout in July.
I tell you what the ones that scare me are the people driving around on bald tires with low pressure and they have no idea. see it every week

That's the most dangerous combination out there, and you're right — most people have no clue. They don't check their tires until something goes wrong. A bald tire with low pressure on a 100-degree day is basically a time bomb.

The underinflation is what really does it. Low pressure means more of the tire contacts the road, which generates more friction, which generates more heat. On a tire that's already got no tread to help cool it down, it doesn't take long.

That's exactly why the free check idea is worth doing. You're literally preventing accidents. And those people become loyal customers because you looked out for them instead of just waiting for them to come in with a flat.
should I be stocking up on anything specific heading into summer? like certain sizes or types that tend to blow more

Good thinking. A few patterns you probably already see:

- **Common SUV and truck sizes** tend to spike in summer because those vehicles are heavier and people load them up for trips. Your 265/70R17s, 275/60R20s — whatever moves most in your area.
- **Budget/economy tires** blow out at a higher rate than mid-range or premium because the rubber compounds and construction aren't as heat-resistant. Worth stocking a few extra mid-range options to upsell people off the cheapest option.
- **Trailer tires** — if you sell any ST tires, summer is when those fail. People pull campers and boats twice a year and never check the tires.

Look at what you replaced most of last July and August. Whatever those sizes and brands were, stock a little deeper on those going in. Better to have them on the shelf than to lose a sale because you're waiting on a shipment.
trailer tires is a good call. I do get a few of those every summer and never have them in stock. always scrambling to order

Yeah, trailer tires are easy money if you have them ready to go. The customer is usually standing there because they need to get back on the road — they're not shopping around. If you've got the tire, you've got the sale at full margin.

Stock a few of the most common ST sizes — ST205/75R15 and ST225/75R15 cover a big chunk of boat and utility trailers. Maybe a couple ST235/80R16 for the bigger rigs. You don't need a huge inventory, just a few of each so you're not caught empty-handed.

Order them in April before the rush. Prices sometimes creep up in summer when demand spikes.
thats real practical man. appreciate it. im gonna put together a list and get my order in early this year

That's the way to do it. Get the stock in early, put that free inspection sign up in May, and you'll be set up for a strong summer. You're thinking about it at the right time — most shops don't start scrambling until the blowouts are already happening.

Good luck putting that order together.
